coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped silicon-carbon dots for fluorescence enhancement and on-site colorimetric detection of dopamine with high selectivity
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yi-Fan
Li, Li
Jiang, Ming
Yang, Xin
Yu, Xu
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: The boron and nitrogen co-doped silicon-carbon dots (BN-SiCDs) were synthesized by one-pot method. The introduction of boron in BN-SiCDs played a significant role to selectively respond to dopamine (DA) based on fluorescence enhancement, while almost no respond to structural analogues, i.e. norepinephrine and epinephrine. The hydrogen bonds and π-π conjugation between BN-SiCDs and DA oligomers, and the polymerization nature of DA could be contributing factors to this phenomenon. BN-SiCDs were employed as the fluorescent nanoprobe to determine DA with the linearity of 0.005–10 μg mL−1, and limit of detection of 1.58 ng mL−1. For on-site detection, the fluorescence colorimetric probe was constructed based on BN-SiCDs and bovine serum albumin-stabilized gold nanoclusters. The sensitive color change from red to blue-green was visually observed by naked eyes corresponding to DA concentration of 0–9 μg mL−1. Further combined with the APP by the smartphone, the colorimetric probe was used for the quantitative detection. The proposed BN-SiCDs probe, colorimetric system or APP method were applied to determine DA in hydrochloride dopamine injection and human urine samples, and results agreed well with each other. The present study provided an effective strategy to adjust properties of fluorescent nanoprobes from synthetic sources to realize high selective detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon-carbon dots
Dopamine
Fluorescence enhancement
Colorimetric probe
On-site detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Material and methods
Materials and instruments
One-pot synthesis of BN-SiCDs
Determination of DA based on fluorescence enhancement using the BN-SiCDs as the probe
Determination of DA in real samples
Colorimetric detection of DA by naked eyes and smartphone
Results and discussion
Optimization of synthesis conditions for BN-SiCDs
Effect of reaction time and APBA doping amounts on fluorescence properties of BN-SiCDs
Effect of reaction time and APBA doping amounts on the BN-SiCDs’ response to catecholamines
Optimization of dialysis time of BN-SiCDs
Characterization of the BN-SiCDs
Highly selective detection of DA based on fluorescence enhancement using BN-SiCDs as the probe
Optimization of DA detection conditions using BN-SiCDs as the probe
Selectivity and interference study of the BN-SiCDs probe towards DA
Mechanism of fluorescence enhancement detection of DA using BN-SiCDs as the probe
Evaluation of the developed method for DA detection
Analysis of DA in dopamine hydrochloride injection and human urine
Colorimetric detection of DA by naked eye and smartphone
Conclusion
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgments
Supplementary material
References

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2019QYTD09), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2020kfyXJJS082) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804105). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for instrumental support.

The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2019QYTD09), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2020kfyXJJS082) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804105). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for instrumental support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Response of the prepared SiCDs under different conditions as named in Table S1 to catecholamines as designed in the respective figures. The experiments were performed as described in Section 2.3. For NP or EP analysis, NP or EP (10 μg mL−1) instead of DA was used following the same operation.
The TEM image (A), particle size distribution (B), FT-IR spectra (C), XPS spectra (D and E), and the stability of the purified BN-SiCDs (e2) towards pH (F), NaCl (G) and UV irradiation (λ = 370 nm).

Selectivity and interference study of the BN-SiCDs probe towards DA. (The experiments were performed as described in Section 2.3. *Ag+ and *Cu2+ represent that the concentration of Ag+ and Cu2+ were 0.2 μg mL−1 and 4 μg mL−1, respectively).

UV absorption spectra (A) and fluorescence spectra (B) of the related substances as designed in the figures. (C) The speculated detection mechanism diagram. (I) the interaction between boric acid group of BN-SiCDs and catecholamine; (II) the interaction between boric acid group of BN-SiCDs and DA oligomers, taking the tetramers of DA [35] as an example; (III) the π-π conjugation between BN-SiCDs and DA oligomers. (D) Photos of the substances as designed in the figures under natural light and UV light at 365 nm. TEM images of BD-SiCDs + DA (E) and BD-SiCDs + DA + Cys (F).

Fluorescence spectra (A) and photos (B) of colorimetric fluorescence sensors to DA solution of different concentrations and diluted injection sample. (C) Linear fit results between B/R value and DA concentration. (D) Screenshot of APP operation process. The experiments were performed as described in Section 2.5.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped silicon-carbon dots for fluorescence enhancement and on-site colorimetric detection of dopamine with high selectivity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The boron and nitrogen co-doped silicon-carbon dots (BN-SiCDs) were synthesized by one-pot method. The introduction of boron in BN-SiCDs played a significant role to selectively respond to dopamine (DA) based on fluorescence enhancement, while almost no respond to structural analogues, i.e. norepinephrine and epinephrine. The hydrogen bonds and π-π conjugation between BN-SiCDs and DA oligomers, and the polymerization nature of DA could be contributing factors to this phenomenon. BN-SiCDs were employed as the fluorescent nanoprobe to determine DA with the linearity of 0.005–10 μg mL−1, and limit of detection of 1.58 ng mL−1. For on-site detection, the fluorescence colorimetric probe was constructed based on BN-SiCDs and bovine serum albumin-stabilized gold nanoclusters. The sensitive color change from red to blue-green was visually observed by naked eyes corresponding to DA concentration of 0–9 μg mL−1. Further combined with the APP by the smartphone, the colorimetric probe was used for the quantitative detection. The proposed BN-SiCDs probe, colorimetric system or APP method were applied to determine DA in hydrochloride dopamine injection and human urine samples, and results agreed well with each other. The present study provided an effective strategy to adjust properties of fluorescent nanoprobes from synthetic sources to realize high selective detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence analysis is superior to other instrumental analytical methods owing to the low cost, simple operation, satisfactory sensitivity, less equipment required, and fast detection speed. However, since few substances possess fluorescence in nature, some strategies have to be adopted to endow analytes with the fluorescence via fluorescence derivatization and fluorescent dye labelling, etc. Recently, a more popular strategy relies on fluorescent probes, through the fluorescence changes of which the target analyte can be indirectly qualitatively or quantitatively analyzed [1,2].

Among fluorescent probes, fluorescent nanomaterials attracted extensive attention, including metal nanoclusters, quantum dots, carbon dots, etc [2,3]. Silicon-carbon dots (SiCDs) were a recently emerging fluorescent nanomaterial which shared the advantages of both silicon quantum dots and carbon dots, including adjustable optical properties, satisfactory chemical and photo stability, excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability, and non- or low-toxicity [3–5]. However, it is still a challenge to generate SiCDs with good water solubility, strong fluorescence and excellent detectability with high sensitivity and selectivity.

Dopamine (DA), as the most abundant catecholamine neurotransmitter in the human brain, plays an important role in regulating various physiological functions of the central nervous system. Abnormal secretion of DA was reported to be related to a variety of diseases, like Parkinson's disease, Huntington’s disease, schizophrenia, depression, attention deficit hyperactivity syndrome and pituitary tumors [6]. In addition, DA can be used as a medicine (such as dopamine hydrochloride injection) for shock syndrome caused by myocardial infarction, trauma, heart surgery, renal failure, congestive heart failure, etc. [7]. Therefore, for the drug quality control and diagnosis of disease, fast and convenient methods for analyzing DA in drugs and biological samples were desired, especially for on-site detection. For this purpose, fluorescence sensor could be a preferred method. Since DA was not fluorescent itself, the fluorescent probe was necessary to establish the related method. So far, various fluorescent probes, including quantum dots, carbon dots, etc., were reported for DA detection [8–17]. However, although the detection selectivity could be enhanced by surface modification, e.g. molecularly imprinted polymer coating [18], it was still highly desired of the conveniently prepared fluorescent probe, especially the one which discriminated DA from the structural analogues, i.e. norepinephrine (NP) and epinephrine (EP). Inspired by the special interaction between boronic acid and the cis-diol group (contained in DA) and self-polymerization properties of DA, we supposed to create a fluorescence sensor based on SiCDs with the B doping, and hopefully it could distinguish the DA from the structural analogues.

In the present study, the aminobenzeneboronic acid (APBA) and 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTMS) were selected as raw sources to generate the boron and nitrogen co-doped silicon-carbon dots (BN-SiCDs) by a simple one-port solvothermal method. The BN-SiCDs had unexpected selectivity, which particularly distinguished DA from structural analogues, i.e. NP and EP. Moreover, the fluorescence colorimetric probe based on BN-SiCDs and bovine serum albumin-stabilized gold nanoclusters (BSA@AuNCs) was constructed for visual semi-quantification of DA. The method could be further combined with the smartphone for the on-site reliable APP detection. The proposed BN-SiCDs could be employed as a satisfactory selective fluorescent probe for fast detection of DA in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material and methods
Materials and instruments
ce:section.ce:para: The details of the materials and instruments were described in the supplementary file.

ce:section.ce:section-title: One-pot synthesis of BN-SiCDs
ce:section.ce:para: BN-SiCDs were prepared via a one-pot solvothermal approach. Generally, APBA of certain amount were dissolved in 8 mL ethanol followed by the addition of 2 mL APTMS, which were then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave of 25 mL, deaerated with nitrogen for 10 min, and heated at 200 °C for the prescribed time. Afterwards, the reaction was stopped by natural cooling down to room temperature, and the brown yellow transparent solution was produced. To purify the resultant product, the solution was subjected to the dialysis with the dialysis membrane of 500 Da for 9 h to remove the unreacted reagents. Thus-obtained purified BN-SiCDs solution was kept at 4 °C for further use.

In order to maximize the detectability of BN-SiCDs towards DA, the synthetic conditions, e.g. different APBA doping amount (0–0.6 g) and reaction time (4–20 h) at the fixed APTMS amount (2 mL), were investigated. For short, the obtained products and their corresponding synthesis conditions are listed in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DA based on fluorescence enhancement using the BN-SiCDs as the probe
ce:section.ce:para: For the determination of DA, briefly, 10 μL of BN-SiCDs, 1.9 mL of PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) and 90 μL of DA sample solution were well mixed with incubating for 30 min. Afterwards, the solution was used for the fluorescence measurement at excitation and emission wavelengths of 415 nm and 467 nm, respectively; thus-obtained fluorescence intensity was named as F. The florescence intensity of BN-SiCDs themselves without the addition of DA was F0. F/F0 was the criterion for quantification.

For linearity test, DA solutions of 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0 and 10.0 μg mL−1 were determined as described above. The linearity between F/F0 and the concentration of DA was therefore obtained for calibration. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated based on 3σ/k (σ was the standard deviation of F0, and k was the slope of the calibration curve).

For the selectivity experiment, BN-SiCDs of 10 μL were dispersed into the each investigated interfering substance aqueous solution (10 μg mL-1), including Na+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Cu2+, CO3 2−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, Glu, NP, EP, L-Try, D-Try, L-His, L-Phe, Met, Ala and BSA. To assess the interference effect, BN-SiCDs of 10 μL were dispersed into DA solution (10 μg mL−1) spiked with one of the above individual interfering substances (10 μg mL−1) separately. The fluorescence intensity of these samples was measured as stated above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine hydrochloride injection was precisely measured and appropriately diluted with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, 20 mM), and then directly used for fluorescence analysis as described above in Section 2.3.

Considering human urine was much complex, the standard addition quantification method was adopted to minimize the sample matrix interference. Briefly, human urine sample was spiked with different concentrations of DA (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0 μg mL−1), and then diluted 10 times with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, 20 mM), for the fluorescence measurement as described in Section 2.3.
To validate the proposed fluorescence method, a modified high performance liquid chromatographic (HPLC) method was used to analyze DA [19] as specified in the supplementary file.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of DA by naked eyes and smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: BSA@AuNCs used in the present study were synthesized according to the reported method [20]. 10 μL of BN-SiCDs, 80 μL BSA@AuNCs and 1.82 mL of PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, 20 mM) were well mixed, and then 90 μL of DA sample solution of respective different concentrations (0–9 μg mL−1) was added and mixed. The resulting solutions were directly observed and photographed under UV light (λ = 365 nm) for colorimetric semi-quantification of DA by naked eyes. For smartphone detection, the photographs were collected by a smartphone, and R/G/B values were read by APP (Color Collect, Version 2.5.1). The relationship between the B/R (the ratio of B and R value) and DA concentration was fitted, and the linearity equation and R2 were displayed.

For visual detection of DA in dopamine hydrochloride injection, the injection was firstly precisely measured and appropriately diluted with PBS solution (pH = 7.4, 20 mM). Then 1.91 mL of the diluted injection solution was added with BN-SiCDs (10 μL), BSA@AuNCs (80 μL) for colorimetric semi-quantification of DA under UV light (λ = 365 nm) by naked eyes and photographed by smartphone as described above. The corresponding R/G/B value of the photo was read through the APP of smartphone, and the B/R value was brought into the fitted equation to calculate the DA concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Optimization of synthesis conditions for BN-SiCDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore fluorescence properties of BN-SiCDs and optimize the detectability towards DA, BN-SiCDs with different APBA doping amount and different reaction time at the fixed APTMS amount were synthesized as listed in Table S1. These BN-SiCDs were evaluated in terms of their fluorescence quantum yields (QYs), fluorescence properties and response to catecholamines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction time and APBA doping amounts on fluorescence properties of BN-SiCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The QYs of the BN-SiCDs were determined with quinine sulfate as reference according to the previous report [21], as listed in Table S1. Apparently, without APBA, the QYs of the obtained SiCDs (a1-a5) were the largest. Once APBA was introduced, the QYs of the BN-SiCDs which were obtained from the same reaction time, decreased sharply from those without APBA addition, and then the decrease was slowing down with the further enhancing APBA doping amount. For example, comparing a2 (QY 35.39%), b2 (QY 23.1%), c2 (QY 15.61%), d2 (QY 13.31%) and e2 (QY 12.80%) with the same reaction time, the QYs decreased about 1/3 from a2 to b2, but the decrease degree of QYs was narrowed further. This result illustrated that the presence of B in the BN-SiCDs would lower QYs of the material, but the actual content of B in the BN-SiCDs may have less effect on QYs, which may be related to the low B doping amount in the final material. As measured by XPS, the B atomic concentration in a2, b2, c2, d2 and e2 were 0%, 0.1%, 0.3%, 0.3% and 0.4%, respectively. However, no matter what amount of APBA was added, the QYs of the resulting material reached the maximum value at 8 h of reaction time. Correspondingly, the appeared color of BN-SiCDs synthesized from different conditions under natural light and UV light (λ = 365 nm) are compared in Fig. S1A. With the same reaction time but the enhancing APBA doping amounts, the color of the product deepened gradually under natural light, from light yellow (without APBA doping) to brown yellow, while the corresponding fluorescence color gradually transitioned from blue (without APBA doping) to blue-green. The fluorescence spectra of the materials were further studied to explain the observation.

The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of BN-SiCDs prepared with different amounts of APBA for 8 h reaction are presented in Fig. S1B. It can be seen that as the APBA doping amount increased from 0 g to 0.6 g, both the excitation and emission wavelength exhibited a light redshift. The excitation peak at ∼230 nm gradually weakened, while the excitation peak at around ∼260 nm gradually became apparent and slight stronger, and the maximum excitation peak red-shifted from ∼360 nm to ∼370 nm. Besides, owing to the introduction of APBA, the maximum emission peak was obviously broadened and weakened with the redshift from ∼440 nm to ∼450 nm, while a new peak at ∼500 nm gradually appeared, which was consistent with the fluorescent color change from blue to blue-green. This phenomenon may reflect the role of B for the longer wavelength fluorescence emission [22], and the redshift herein may be closely related to the variation of surface trap states within the optical bandgap of the SiCDs with and without B-doping [22].

Fig. S1C depicts the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of BN-SiCDs prepared from different reaction times with the same APBA doping amount. For e1, the excitation peak at ∼300 nm was abnormally high, which may be related to the reaction intermediate. When the APBA doping amount was constant, the reaction time had almost no effect on the maximum excitation (λ = 370 nm) and emission (λ = 450 nm) wavelength position of BN-SiCDs (except e1), but had an obvious effect on the fluorescence intensity with the strongest at 8 h reaction.

The above analysis demonstrated that the introduction of B through APBA doping caused some changes in the fluorescence properties of the resulting BN-SiCDs, including a slight redshift of the maximum excitation and emission wavelength, the appearance of new emission peak at longer wavelength and the decrease of QY. It was worth mentioning that when the B doping amount in the SiCDs reached a certain level, further enhancing the APBA amount in the raw reactants would have little effect on the B content in the final product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction time and APBA doping amounts on the BN-SiCDs’ response to catecholamines
ce:section.ce:para: The BN-SiCDs obtained under different conditions as listed in Table S1 were individually attempted as the probe to study their respective response to catecholamines, as displayed in Fig. 1 . In Fig. 1A, the three studied catecholamines quenched all the fluorescence of a1-a5 (without B doping), and the fluorescence quenching degree by DA was stronger than the other two catecholamines, i.e. NP and EP. In addition, a1-a5 exhibited little responsive variance towards DA. The result indicated that it was hard to tell the three catecholamines of similar structures by the quenching responses based on SiCDs as the probe.

Conversely, once the BN-SiCDs with B doping, no matter which was obtained from different reaction conditions, were attempted as the probe, the fluorescence enhancement towards catecholamines was observed, especially obviously in the case of DA, as displayed in Fig. 1B-F. Although some variant trends were noted, the fluorescence enhancement towards DA gradually strengthened as BN-SiCDs obtained with the increase of APBA doping amount, except in Fig. 1B. However, when the APBA doping amount remained constant, the maximum response of BN-SiCDs to DA mostly occurred at the reaction time of 8 h.

Comparing Fig. 1B-F with Fig. 1A, obviously, the introduction of APBA greatly enhanced the selectivity of the probe in response to DA. DA quenched the fluorescence of a1-a5, while, in contrast, DA enhanced the fluorescence of other prepared BN-SiCDs. Considering that fluorescence quenching effect was more prevalent than the fluorescence enhancing effect [8–17], the observed phenomenon could be relatively specific to the DA detection. Hence, the BN-SiCDs with the APBA doping amount of 0.6 g and the reaction time of 8 h, i.e. e2, were chosen as the probe to specify the DA based on the fluorescence enhancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of dialysis time of BN-SiCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The BN-SiCDs were purified by dialysis, and the dialysis time was optimized based on the fluorescence response of BN-SiCDs to catecholamines as shown in Fig. S2. With the extension of dialysis time, the fluorescence response of the resulting purified BN-SiCDs to DA firstly increased and then decreased, but the response to NP and EP remained almost unchanged. The reason may be that in the early stage of dialysis, a large amount of unreacted small molecules, like APBA and APTMS, etc., were dialyzed out, so that the interference to the DA detection was reduced and the fluorescence enhancement was enlarged. However, with the further dialyzing, the dialysis of small molecules may be relatively complete while the loss of BN-SiCDs became serious, which in turn led to the decrease of the response by using the same volume of BN-SiCDs for DA detection. Therefore, the dialysis time of 9 h was selected for purifying the BN-SiCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the BN-SiCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The above optimized BN-SiCDs, i.e. e2, was characterized by TEM, FI-IR, XPS and stability tests. The BN-SiCDs were observed to be spherical and almost monodisperse by TEM in Fig. 2 A. According to the TEM image, the particle size distribution of BN-SiCDs ranged from 1.4 nm to 2.6 nm with an average size of 2.0 nm (Fig. 2B). Besides, the high-resolution TEM image (inset in Fig. 2A) reflected that BN-SiCDs possessed the crystalline structure with an interlayer lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, possibly indicating the similar structure of BN-SiCDs to graphene stacks [23,24].

Fig. 2C displayed the typical FT-IR spectra of SiCDs and BN-SiCDs. The absorption peaks at 3360 cm−1 and 1593 cm−1 were assigned to the bending vibration of NH [25], the peak at 748–802 cm−1 was resulted from the wagging vibration of NH [26], the broad peak at ∼1115 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of SiOSi [27], and those at 1475 cm−1 and 2928 cm−1 belonged to CH bending and CH unsaturated stretching vibration, respectively [28]. The stretching vibration and deformation of OH [29] appeared as the absorption peaks in the range of 2830–2900 cm−1. The above results demonstrated that the synthesized SiCDs and BN-SiCDs were rich in amino and hydroxyl groups.

The XPS measurement was conducted to evaluate the elemental composition of the prepared BN-SiCDs. In Fig. 2D by the full range analysis of XPS, the peaks at 102.3 eV, 149.0 eV, 192.0 eV, 284.8 eV, 398.9 eV and 532.1 eV corresponded to Si 2p, Si 2s, B 1s, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s respectively. The atomic concentration of Si, B, C, N and O in BN-SiCDs were 15.1%, 0.4%, 50.1%, 12.9% and 21.5%, respectively. Further analysis of B 1s spectrum in Fig. 2E disclosed that two distinct boron atoms, i.e. BC (190.8 eV) and BO (192.1 eV), were present, which could be an indication of the status of B in BN-SiCDs.

The stability of BN-SiCDs towards the surrounding pH, NaCl and UV irradiation (λ = 370 nm) were reflected in Fig. 2F, G and H. In Fig. 2F, the fluorescence intensity of BN-SiCDs obviously weakened from pH 7 to 9, but remained relatively stable under acidic (pH < 7) and basic (pH > 9) conditions. The changes of fluorescence intensity may be related to the ionization status of surface groups of BN-SiCDs responding to different environmental pHs, such as OH, B(OH)2 and NH2 [30]. As well, the stability of the BN-SiCDs towards NaCl of different concentrations (0–1.0 M) and UV irradiation was satisfactory as indicated in Fig. 2G and H, which was appreciated in the subsequent applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Highly selective detection of DA based on fluorescence enhancement using BN-SiCDs as the probe
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed in Section 3.1.2, BN-SiCDs were found to be specific for DA with the remarkable fluorescence enhancement. Hence, this nanomaterial was investigated as the fluorescence probe to detect DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of DA detection conditions using BN-SiCDs as the probe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain the optimal conditions for detecting DA by BN-SiCDs, the effect of pH (Fig. S3A), NaCl (Fig. S3B), BN-SiCDs dosage (Fig. S3C) and incubation time (Fig. S3D) on the response of BN-SiCDs to DA were studied. The optimized detection condition was obtained as described in Section 2.3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference study of the BN-SiCDs probe towards DA
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is essential for the reliability of analytical methods, which can verify the practical applicability of the proposed method. The selectivity of the developed fluorescent probe towards DA was thus herein studied. The potential interfering substances, including inorganic ions (Na+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Cu2+, CO3 2−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −) and organic substances (Glu, NP, EP, L-Try, D-Try, L-His, L-Phe, Met, Ala, BSA), were chosen for selectivity and interference study. As displayed in Fig. 3 , the above studied substances had almost no influence on the fluorescence of BN-SiCDs when they were individually incubated with BN-SiCDs; when each of these substances coexisted with DA to be incubated with BN-SiCDs, except Ag+ and Cu2+, less fluorescence change was observed for the case in the presence of only DA and BN-SiCDs. Although Ag+ and Cu2+ exhibited some interference to DA detection at comparable concentration, both ions may interfere the detection to a less degree. For the dopamine hydrochloride injection, Ag+ and Cu2+ do not coexist with DA. Additionally, the content of DA in healthy human body is much higher than that of Ag+ and Cu2+. For example, the content of DA in the human urine of normal people is about 50–100 times of Ag+ and 2.5–10 times of Cu2+ [31,32]. Thus, the interference experiment when DA coexisted with Ag+ (50 times less) and Cu2+ (2.5 times less) of different concentrations were also performed, as yellow column in Fig. 3. When the concentration of DA was larger than that of Ag+ and Cu2+, the interference of Ag+ and Cu2+ could be negligible, which implied the suitability of the probe for urine analysis. Anyway, Ag+ and Cu2+ could be firstly removed by alkaline precipitation [33], while the alkaline environment did not affect the response of BN-SiCDs to DA (as depicted in Fig. S3A); this could be another strategy to solve the probem. The results in Fig. 3 indicated that the proposed probe possessed superior selectivity. Even the structural analogues to DA, e.g. NP and EP, had no interference on the detection of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence enhancement detection of DA using BN-SiCDs as the probe
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed in Section 3.1.2, BN-SiCDs exhibited the specific fluorescence enhancement response to DA than the other two catecholamines. To explore the mechanism for this observation, the UV and fluorescence spectra of BN-SiCDs and BN-SiCDs interacted with DA (BN-SiCDs + DA) are compared in Fig. 4 A and B, respectively. In Fig. 4A, compared to BN-SiCDs, UV absorption of BN-SiCDs + DA at ∼290 nm was significantly enhanced with the appearance of a new broad absorption peak at 350–600 nm, implying the formation of a new complex between BN-SiCDs and DA [23]. This could be ascribed to a variety of forces between BN-SiCDs and DA, such as the hydrogen bond between NH2, OH, etc. on the surface of BN-SiCDs and NH2, OH of DA, and the π-π conjugation between them. In addition, the presence of B in BN-SiCDs played an irreplaceable role for the high selectivity towards DA as demonstrated in Fig. 1. As known, a five-membered ester ring may be expected to be formed between the boronic acid group of BN-SiCDs and the cis-diol group of DA or DA oligomers, which could lead to the formation of the larger conjugate rigid plane structure [34], as depicted in Fig. 4C (I) and (II). Additionally, as indicated in the inset in Fig. 2A, the BN-SiCDs were supposed to possess a sheet plane structure similar to graphene stacks [23,24]. DA, especially DA oligomers, are also rigid planar molecules [35], which can attach to the surface of the BN-SiCDs via π-π conjugation as speculated in Fig. 4C (III), further enhancing the rigidity of the material [24]. As a result, owing to the presence of larger conjugate rigid plane structure between BN-SiCDs and DA or DA oligomers, the energy loss of DA or DA oligomers by molecular vibration or rotation in the excited state may be minimized, and the excited state energy was thus more likely to be released via fluorescence radiation, leading to the fluorescence enhancement [24]. This also explained the reason contributing to the observation that the response of SiCDs to catecholamine changed from quenching without B doping to enhancement after APBA was introduced. Moreover, the redshift of the maximum excitation wavelength and a little blueshift of emission wavelength of BN-SiCDs + DA further implied the formation of new substances (Fig. 4B). This also explained the reason for the detection of DA at the excitation (415 nm) and emission (467 nm) wavelengths instead of the maximum excitation (370 nm) and emission (450 nm) wavelengths of BN-SiCDs.

In addition, from Fig. S3A, the response of BN-SiCDs towards DA was significantly strengthened under alkaline condition. DA was well known to prone to polymerize under alkaline conditions [36]. Reasonably, it was speculated that the pronounced fluorescence enhancement of BN-SiCDs by DA may be related to the self-polymerization of DA (Fig. 4C (II) and (III)). This could be a contributing reason for the higher selectivity of BN-SiCDs towards DA than the other two analogues, since polymerization of DA was much easier than EP and NP [37]. To testify this speculation, the response of BN-SiCDs to DA in the presence of Cys was investigated, as Cys was claimed to inhibit the polymerization of DA [38]. As depicted in Fig. 4A, in the presence of Cys, the UV absorption at 290 nm weakened, and the broad peak at 350 nm ∼ 600 nm was significantly suppressed. Moreover, the fluorescence of BN-SiCDs, BN-SiCDs + DA, and the mixture of BN-SiCDs, DA and Cys (BN-SiCDs + DA + Cys) exposed to natural light and ultraviolet light (Fig. 4D) were compared. Obviously, the presence of Cys inhibited the fluorescence enhancement largely. Hence, the early-polymerization of DA to form DA oligomers could be a contributing reason for the specific detectability of the developed probe.

In order to further prove the speculation, BN-SiCDs + DA and BN-SiCDs + DA + Cys were observed by TEM. The obvious aggregates were observed in the case of BN-SiCDs + DA in Fig. 4E, which may be induced by the polymerization of DA. As a comparison in Fig. 4F, the material was more dispersed for BN-SiCDs + DA + Cys, which could be due to the addition of Cys, inhibiting the polymerization of DA and possibly thus the aggregation of BN-SiCDs, which agreed well with images in Fig. 2A. As another demonstration, DLS analysis was carried out. For only BN-SiCDs in PBS, there was no effective hydration particle size values provided by the DLS analysis, which may be because of the smaller size than the detection limit of the instrument (∼10 nm). For BN-SiCDs + DA in PBS, the hydrated particle size could be detected by DLS. However, when only DA was present but without BN-SiCDs, it took 8–9 fold times longer to reach a comparable size. Besides, the hydrated particle size was also unavailable for BN-SiCDs + DA + Cys, which was consistent with the result of TEM. Both TEM and DLS results indicated the aggregation of BN-SiCDs caused by polymerization of DA, which may lead to the increasing local concentration of BN-SiCDs, thus resulting in fluorescence enhancement, like a kind of aggregation-induced fluorescence emission [39]. In addition, in the experiment, BN-SiCDs were found to accelerate the polymerization of DA, but without the generation of black polydopamine precipitate. The reasons for this phenomenon could be explained as follows. It was reported that in the process of DA polymerization and deposition, covalent bonds played a leading role in the initial stage (e.g. the formation of oligomers), while non-covalent bonds played a dominant role in the later stage [40]. However, in this case, the indole structure, amino and carboxylic acid groups, o-diphenol or quinone groups in DA oligomers may interact with BN-SiCDs through hydrogen bonding and π-π conjugation as discussed above, thereby the further deposition of DA oligomers was inhibited.

To further explore the role of BN-SiCDs as an oxidant or catalyst in the process of accelerating DA polymerization, the response of BN-SiCDs to DA in the presence or absence of O2 was compared in Fig. 4B and D. Obviously, only in the presence of O2, DA could cause the seriously enhanced fluorescence of BN-SiCDs, otherwise, the response was prohibited. The observation demonstrated that BN-SiCDs could catalyze the oxidative polymerization of DA, but exerted no obvious ability to oxidize DA. This could be a reason for the relative fast detection of DA as BN-SiCDs accelerated the early-polymerization of DA to form DA oligomers. In order to verify the speculation, polymerization of DA was carried out in PBS (pH = 9), as photographed in Fig. S4(A1-A6). During the polymerization, the solution was sampled at the prescribed time and then for the analysis by the BN-SiCDs probe as depicted in Fig. S4B. The results indicated that, during the process of gradual polymerization of DA to form polydopamine (PDA) from Fig. S4 A1 to A6, the response of BN-SiCDs to the same amount of sampled reaction solution was constant. To be noted was that the black PDA precipitation immediately disappeared once the sampled reaction solution contacted with BN-SiCDs. It has been reported that DA oligomers aggregated to form PDA precipitation through non-covalent bonding force [40]. Once BN-SiCDs were added, the interaction between BN-SiCDs and DA oligomers may be stronger than that between DA oligomers themselves, resulting in the depolymerization of PDA into DA oligomers, so that PDA precipitation disappeared, which was consistent with the above hypothesis that BN-SiCDs accelerated early-polymerization of DA to form oligomers but without further deposition. This phenomenon further proved that the fluorescence enhancement of BN-SiCDs was induced by the DA oligomers (as shown in Fig. 4B).

To sum up, the reason for the high selective fluorescence enhancement of BN-SiCDs by DA mainly came from two aspects. On the one hand, DA was more likely to polymerize than EP and NP, and the presence of BN-SiCDs was found to accelerate the polymerization of DA to form oligomers in the initial stage without further deposition. On the other hand, DA oligomers could interact with BN-SiCDs through hydrogen bonds and π-π conjugation, and thus caused the aggregation of BN-SiCDs, resulting in fluorescence enhancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the developed method for DA detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under the best detection conditions, the response of BN-SiCDs to DA of different concentrations is displayed in Fig. S5. It was found that the fluorescence intensity gradually strengthened with the elevated DA concentration. A good linear relationship was established between F/F0 and the concentration of DA in the range of 0.005–10 μg mL−1 with the linear fitting equation of y = 4.0523x + 1.008 and R2 = 0.9968. The LOD was 1.58 ng mL−1. Compared with other reported methods as listed in Table S2, our developed method provided lower or comparable LOD and a wider linear range. Particularly, our probe offered superior selectivity to differentiate DA from the three structural analogs catecholamines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of DA in dopamine hydrochloride injection and human urine
ce:section.ce:para: In order to apply the developed method to actual samples, DA in the dopamine hydrochloride injection and human urine were analyzed as listed in Table S3. The sample matrix of the dopamine hydrochloride injection was simple, and no sample matrix effect was observed on the developed sensing method. Hence, the established quantification method in Section 3.3.4 could be directly applied for quantification of DA in it. The concentration of DA in the diluted injection sample (Section 2.4) was determined to be 0.989 μg mL−1, and thus DA in injection was calculated to be 9.89 mg mL−1, which was consistent with the labelled amount (10 mg mL−1) and within the range of the required content in Chinese pharmacopeia (9.3–10.7 mg mL−1). Also, HPLC analysis of DA in the injection was calculated to be 9.87 mg mL−1, which further demonstrated the reliability of the developed fluorescence analysis based on the BN-SiCDs as the probe.

As for human urine sample, considering the complex sample matrix, the standard addition method was used to analyze DA as described in Section 2.4. The standard addition linearity equation was y = 4.0514x + 1.1313 with R2 of 0.9980. The DA in the treated urine samples was determined to be 0.0324 μg mL−1, thus the concentration of DA in urine was calculated to be 0.324 μg mL−1, which agreed well with the result (0.316 μg mL−1) by HPLC analysis and lied in the routine DA concentration in urine samples of healthy subjects [41], and the recovery rate was between 90.9 and 101.5%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of DA by naked eye and smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, colorimetric sensors have attracted much attention because of their low cost, simple operation, fast detection and no need of expensive equipment. For the colorimetric sensing system, the visual detection of the target substance can be realized based on the color change of the detection system, and thus the naked eyes can be directly used to replace the instrument for determination, which not only saves the cost but also provides a new idea for on-site detection [42,43].

For the on-site detection of DA, the colorimetric probe was constructed. Taking into account that the fluorescence enhancement of BN-SiCDs by DA was blue-green light, the red fluorescent BSA@AuNCs were deliberately selected to achieve visual detection of DA by naked eyes since its red fluorescence could be quenched by DA [20]. Fig. 5 A shows the fluorescence emission spectra of DA of different concentrations in the presence of BN-SiCDs and BSA@AuNCs (1:8, v:v), which was the optimal ratio of the colorimetric probe to embody the sensitive color change with the DA concentration (data not shown). As the concentration of DA increased, the fluorescence intensity at 470 nm gradually strengthened, which corresponded to the fluorescence enhancement of BN-SiCDs by DA; while the fluorescence intensity at 660 nm gradually weakened, corresponding to fluorescence quenching of BSA@AuNCs by DA. This phenomenon demonstrated that the constructed colorimetric probe provided the ideal visual colorimetric conditions for the detection of DA with a rich and apparent color change. As mirrored in Fig. 5B, with the increasing DA from 0 μg mL−1 to 9 μg mL−1, the fluorescence color of the detection system gradually changed from red (0 μg mL−1) to orange-red (0.5 μg mL−1), orange (1–2 μg mL−1), yellow-green (3–4 μg mL−1), green (5–6 μg mL−1), and then blue-green (7–9 μg mL−1).

The operation flow of APP is displayed in Fig. 5D. The R, G, B value of each sample in Fig. 5B was firstly analyzed by the APP. According to Fig. 5A, the changes of emission peaks mainly concentrated at ∼470 nm and ∼660 nm, corresponding to the B value and R value, respectively, thus the relationship of B/R and the DA concentration was analyzed as graphed in Fig. 5C. It can be seen that the B/R value gradually increased as the DA concentration increased from 0 μg mL−1 to 9 μg mL−1, but with the different linearity at 0–4 μg mL−1 and at 4–9 μg mL−1 as listed in Fig. 5C, respectively.

When the diluted injection sample was mixed with the probe proportionally as described in Section 2.5, the fluorescence color of the sample was orange-red (as the inset in Fig. 5B), which was close to the fluorescence color of the 1 μg mL−1 standard sample. The R, G, B values of the sample were then analyzed with smartphone, and the B/R value was calculated to be 0.571. As the concentration of the diluted injection sample (Section 2.5) located at 0–4 μg mL−1, the fitting equation of y = 0.1570x + 0.4162 was thus adopted to calculate the DA concentration to be 0.986 μg mL−1. Both the naked eyes and APP (smartphone) analysis results were consistent with the fluorescence detection results in Section 3.3.5, which demonstrated the reliability of the established colorimetric method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, BN-SiCDs were obtained via a one-step solvothermal reaction by using 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane and aminobenzeneboronic acid as source materials. The introduction of B element caused the redshift of excitation and emission wavelengths and the decrease of QYs of the resulting BN-SiCDs. Especially, the fluorescence enhancement of BN-SiCDs by DA was observed with the distinguishing ability towards the structural analogues (norepinephrine and epinephrine), which was adopted for the selective detection of DA. The mechanism behind the phenomenon was that BN-SiCDs could accelerate the oxidation of DA to form oligomers but without precipitation, and interact with them via hydrogen bond and π-π conjugation, while the early-polymerization of DA to form DA oligomers was also crucial for the selectivity and sensitivity. Moreover, a colorimetric fluorescent probe based on the BN-SiCDs and bovine serum albumin-stabilized gold nanoclusters was established for visual semi-quantification of DA by fluorescent color change. With the further combination with the smartphone, the method could be used for the on-site APP detection. The proposed BN-SiCDs could be employed as a satisfactory selective fluorescent probe for fast detection of DA in real samples. Additionally, the proper element doping from the reaction source could be an effective strategy to adjust the selectivity of the SiCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Yi-Fan Wang: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Li Li: Methodology. Ming Jiang: Writing – original draft. Xin Yang: Validation. Xu Yu: Visualization, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Li Xu: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2019QYTD09), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2020kfyXJJS082) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804105). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for instrumental support.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary material
